input_text:
Voyager 1 embarked on its mission from Cape Canaveral on September 5, 1977. Although both probes were launched using a Titan IIIE-Centaur rocket, Voyager 1 was deployed sixteen days after Voyager 2, notwithstanding the distinct flight plans provided by NASA. The principal objective of this undertaking was to gather data for a duration of five years, during which both Jupiter and Saturn were to undergo intense observation. This periodic alignment of these distant planets, occurring approximately every 176 years, represented a rare and desirable astronomical occurrence.

Voyager 1 encountered Jupiter on March 5, 1979. During this transit, the probe transmitted the first high-resolution imagery of Jupiter's tenuous rings and documented the vigorous volcanism of Io, thereby creating the first documented evidence of volcanism beyond Earth. Two years later, on November 12, 1980, Voyager 1 executed its closest approach to Saturn. It gathered extremely detailed information about Saturn’s ring system and successfully pictured Titan. This voyage past Saturn propelled Voyager 1 past the boundaries of the solar system, bringing its planetary tour to a close and setting a trajectory into interstellar space.

Following a suggestion from astronomer Carl Sagan, Voyager 1 aimed its instruments back toward Earth on February 14, 1990, transmitting one final photograph while it was roughly 6 billion kilometers distant. This solitary image, appearing as a single pixel against the sunlight, became famously known as the Pale Blue Dot. This transmission constituted the last photograph sent by the spacecraft before the cameras were powered down to conserve energy.

Voyager 1 officially entered interstellar space on August 25, 2012, upon breaching the heliopause boundary, making it the first human-made object to achieve this status. It carries the Golden Record, a 12-inch, gold-plated copper phonograph disc assembled following Sagan’s advice. This record includes greetings in 55 languages, alongside audiovisual material intended for any civilization that might eventually find it. Its power supply comes from three radioisotope thermoelectric generators powered by plutonium-238; the gradual decay of this isotope, which has a half-life of 87.7 years, provides the necessary energy.

As of early 2026, Voyager 1 is situated approximately 25 billion kilometers from Earth while maintaining a speed nearing 61,000 kilometers per hour. In November 2026, it will reach the significant milestone of having traveled a distance equal to one light-day.
output_text:
Voyager 1 began its mission from Cape Canaveral on September 5, 1977. Although both probes utilized a Titan IIIE-Centaur rocket for launch, Voyager 1 was deployed sixteen days after Voyager 2, despite the differing flight plans provided by NASA. The primary goal of this endeavor was to collect data for a span of five years, dedicated to intensive observation of both Jupiter and Saturn. The alignment of these faraway planets, which happens roughly every 176 years, was an infrequent and valuable astronomical event.

Voyager 1 arrived at Jupiter on March 5, 1979. During this passage, the probe relayed the first high-resolution images of Jupiter's faint rings and documented the intense volcanism on Io, thus providing the initial evidence of volcanism beyond Earth. Two years subsequently, on November 12, 1980, Voyager 1 performed its closest pass to Saturn. It gathered extremely detailed data regarding Saturn's ring system and successfully imaged Titan. This journey past Saturn propelled Voyager 1 beyond the solar system's limits, concluding its planetary tour and setting a course into interstellar space.

Following a suggestion from astronomer Carl Sagan, Voyager 1 aimed its instruments back toward Earth on February 14, 1990, transmitting a final photograph while it was approximately 6 billion kilometers away. This solitary picture, appearing as a solitary pixel against sunlight, became famously known as the Pale Blue Dot. This transmission marked the last photograph sent by the spacecraft before the cameras were shut down to conserve power.

Voyager 1 officially entered interstellar space on August 25, 2012, upon crossing the heliopause boundary, making it the first human-made object to achieve this status. It carries the Golden Record, a 12-inch, gold-plated copper phonograph disc assembled based on Sagan's counsel. This record includes greetings in 55 languages, along with audiovisual material intended for any civilization that might eventually locate it. Its power supply originates from three radioisotope thermoelectric generators fueled by plutonium-238; the gradual decay of this isotope, which has a half-life of 87.7 years, supplies the required energy.

As of early 2026, Voyager 1 is situated about 25 billion kilometers from Earth while maintaining a velocity approaching 61,000 kilometers per hour. In November 2026, it will reach the noteworthy milestone of having traversed a distance equivalent to one light-day.
